seo_title: CAPS: Efficient Parallel Reasoning for Large Language Models
meta_description: CASCaded Adaptive Pairwise Selection (CAPS) improves parallel reasoning in large language models, reducing verifier-token costs and increasing accuracy.
body_html:
CAPS (Cascaded Adaptive Pairwise Selection) is an inference-only framework that allocates verifier compute non-uniformly along two orthogonal axes: an evidence axis and a distribution axis. This approach reduces the cost of pairwise self-verification in large language models, allowing for more efficient parallel reasoning.

Key Benefits

  • Reduces verifier-token costs by roughly half compared to uniform full-evidence schedules.
  • Increases accuracy in parallel reasoning tasks.
  • Adapts to different problem types, including code and math benchmarks.

Performance on Benchmarks

BenchmarkCAPS Performance
LiveCodeBench-v5/v6 (code)25.4% of verifier-token budget used.
AIME 2025 (math)Outperforms pointwise self-verification on all 20 suites.
CodeContestsOutperforms leading pairwise verifier on 14 of 20 suites.

Trade-offs and Limitations

The trade-off suites admit an interpretable diagnostic in terms of the verifier's accuracy at partial versus full evidence, providing a concrete pre-deployment check for cascade suitability.

While CAPS shows promise in reducing verifier-token costs and increasing accuracy, further research is needed to fully understand its limitations and potential applications.

source.title: CAPS: Cascaded Adaptive Pairwise Selection for Efficient Parallel Reasoning
source.summary: Parallel reasoning, where a generator samples many candidate solutions and an aggregator selects the best, is one of the most effective forms of test-time scaling in large language models, and pairwise self-verification has become its strongest aggregation primitive. Yet pairwise verification carries a heavy cost: each judgment reads two complete solutions in full, and existing methods perform tens of such judgments per problem regardless of whether the comparison is informative. We introduce CAPS (Cascaded Adaptive Pairwise Selection), an inference-only framework that allocates verifier compute non-uniformly along two orthogonal axes: an evidence axis that adapts how much of each candidate the judge sees, and a distribution axis that adapts how comparisons are spread across the pool. CAPS instantiates these into a four-stage cascade with an optional rescue subroutine, and admits a closed-form verifier-token cost in which the per-candidate marginal cost is roughly halved relative to uniform full-evidence schedules. On four self-verifying models (Qwen3-14B, GPT-OSS-20B, Qwen3-4B-Instruct/Thinking) and five reasoning benchmarks spanning code (LiveCodeBench-v5/v6, CodeContests) and math (AIME 2025, HMMT 2025), CAPS outperforms the leading pairwise verifier on 14 of 20 suites while using 25.4% of its verifier-token budget on code, and outperforms pointwise self-verification on all 20. The trade-off suites admit an interpretable diagnostic in terms of the verifier's accuracy at partial versus full evidence, providing a concrete pre-deployment check for cascade suitability.
